speaker: interviewer
text: Everyone shakes when they hold it. You can't help it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I shake anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're actually very steady.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Can you read it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 15, 1996. The survivor's name is Esther Kaufman, E-S-T-H-E-R K-A-U-F-M-A-N. Her maiden name was Zuckierman, Z-U-C-K-I-E-R-M-A-N. The interviewer's name is Jody Dales, J-O-D-Y D-A-L-E-S. We are in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. And the language being spoken is English. This is tape 1.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You need it? OK, he wants you to ask questions, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I look on her?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Jody Dales. Today's date is August 15, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Mrs. Esther Kaufman. This interview is being conducted in English. And we are in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Good afternoon. Could you please tell us your name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Esther Kaufman, and I live in Vancouver. And Kaufman is K-A-U-F-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Esther?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther, E-S-T-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther Zuckierman, Z-U-C-K-I-E-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: October the 11th, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kaufman, if you don't mind, how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I-- 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the city and country in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. And the city is Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-K-- no, C-R-A-K-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Josef.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. J-O-S-E-F, Josef.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he own a business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he had a wholesale in, in Cracow, textile wholesale.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lola, L-O-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your mother's maiden name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rakowska. R-A-K-O-W-S-K-A Rakowska, I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what was she doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was working with my father in the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a successful business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very successful, a wholesale of textile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe their religious--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to the synagogue. My father was too busy go in the weekdays, so he was going the weekends. Like Saturday, he was in the synagogue and every holidays. And my mother was with him. My mother really was more religious than my father. But he love his wife, so he did whatever she wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have one left, Norman, who lives in Calgary. And he-- and I lost three-- two, two brothers I lost during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names, Mrs. Kaufman?

speaker: interviewee
text: One name was Israel. And the other one was Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, were you attending school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- the public school. And after, I went to business school. But it was interrupted by the Germans who were-- who came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what the last grade completed was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completed? The business school wasn't. It was about two, three years I was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a religious school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, not a religious school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they all disappear. There's nobody left from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience any acts of anti-Semitism while you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, during-- before the war, no I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what were your dreams or aspirations as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, in Poland, you-- we were so protected that I couldn't have aspiration because-- I had aspiration to God that my mother and father would be healthy and everything would be-- goes smoothly. My two brothers got married and had grandchildren. We were a very close family. But the aspiration were not there because-- I don't know why. I can see my grandchildren growing up now. And it is completely different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living in your household previous to the Nazi occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was living? We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: After?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with my parents, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers live with you as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brothers both were married. They were separate apartments in different districts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they within walking distance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Cracow was in the walking distance, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were very knit-- close family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any of your other family relatives often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- my-- can I? My, my father had a brother who lived in Cracow. And I was seeing him quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, That's a good question. I really don't remember, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents lived in Vancouver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vancouver, Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vancouver, Canada. And they're buried in the Schara Tzedeck Cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that usual for someone from Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but my mother's whole family lived in Canada, in Vancouver. She was the only one. But she married, and she was very successful. So-- and here, business was very bad. As a matter of fact, she was sending money and whatever she had-- she could help it-- to the parents here, to Vancouver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when they moved from Poland to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it must have been the '20-- not in-- maybe before, '14, maybe, 1914, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have an opportunity to visit them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother promised me when I would be certain age we could go. But never, never came that age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a typical day in your household when you were a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can. Get up in the morning. I-- when I was going to public school, I was rush, rush, rush to the school. And after that was in the afternoon. So we were coming home for lunch because it wasn't too far to go, and the-- when I was taking the business school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was the afternoon, in the evening. So and after that, we are going home. We were close. We didn't have the freedom what the kids have now. My coming was-- my mother and father expected me to be home. Did you have any hobbies or interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I was very guarded, spoiled.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in your spare time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just going out with the kids, and sometimes going places where my parents didn't know where I went. I hope they aren't in another life to see the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of places would you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just walking with boys, but under the bridge where nobody can see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of boyfriends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did most of your girlfriends have lots of boyfriends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we all were, you know, a group of kids going together. But the parents didn't-- they, they shouldn't have done.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe yourself as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quiet. When I had to answer, I had to answer-- respect for the mother and father, and [SIGHS] lots of friends. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had a problem in school, who were you most likely to go to discuss it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father. They would both know my-- if there was a problem, then they knew about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a typical Shabbos for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was quite religious because we had the Jewish maid, who was cooking that while mother was in the business from 8 o'clock in the morning until late, and eating late, late dinner. And Shabbos was-- Friday night dinner was, you know, lighting the candles, and-- a typically religious home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things would you eat for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fish, soup, meat, and vegetables, and that's it, whatever-- pastry.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day, would you go to synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [SIGHS] didn't feel like to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when you grow up, you have something else in mind than to go the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do something else instead?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going with my friends. We were going out. But there was a respect for my mother and father. And when they were coming home, I tried to be home, so-- to give them the respect what they were entitled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers-- did either of your brothers have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- yes, this-- yeah, the oldest brother had one little girl, five years old. And the non-Jewish people were hiding her during the war. And the, the youngest had a baby born in the ghetto. And the German woman killed it, [SOBBING] came with a gun and killed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The oldest brother-- what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember the name of his child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she is alive. She lives in Lugano, Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I visit her-- visit her quite often. Every Sunday, I am phoning her, best sweet girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anne, in Polish was Hanna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the younger brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is gone. He went with my-- from the ghetto, from-- with my mother and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of his daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: He has a son, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shalom. The rabbi didn't want to come to the birth the other way, if they didn't call him Shalom, 'peace.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family-- extended family celebrate High Holidays together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No really. No, because we are a big family. And in Poland, we lived-- we had apartment with three bedrooms. And that was sufficient for my-- for the family. But holidays, for dinner, they were invited-- we had together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that your father's business was successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you consider your family to have been wealthy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very wealthy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy any privileges that other children didn't? Did you go on holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have, yes. I was going with my mother because the only time what she could go was really holiday times. And we were going places where she could observe her holiday. So she was always taking me with her. I was bored because I was the only one daughter. So they always protected me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would you go on your holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- like, in a place where she could really have baths and-- like, you know, you go here, and those places to-- like a spa.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have occasion to travel out of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, my mother wouldn't let me. I was too protected to go to those-- mmm. I had to be certain time at home and not later than 9 o'clock, at 9:30 the latest. And the other way-- and I wasn't punished because I wasn't the baby anymore. But I-- the respect was there. My mother was coming to the house-- if I was sitting, I got up. My father, the same thing because the respect was really for the mother and father, for the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your friends and your parents' friends Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if your father ever encountered any acts of anti-Semitism through his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- if he did, I really don't know. Because he was traveling for his business. Like, he was going to different cities, buying the merchandise, or doing merchandise. Maybe he did. But we were never discussing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you responsible for any household chores?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any youth movements or organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy going to the movies?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going, but not too often. There was one or two movies around, close, Because too far, I was so overprotected, I couldn't go. And, yeah, I was going to movies Sundays.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friends, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start to sense that things were changing, that war was approaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew war is coming. But we didn't expect from the German that. Because I knew there is lots of anti-Semitism in Poland. But I didn't know that that would be the good part, and the German after, wish they could-- I remember like now. When they came in, I was standing before the door. And we lived in an apartment with, like, apartment doors. And we were greeting them. We were so happy that they came, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why were you happy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was a big anti-Semitism in Poland. Some-- like my father, who was a businessman and traveled a lot, so for him, it was a relief. Maybe now it would be better. He was still scared. I knew that only from discussion because of myself, I didn't feel nothing. I didn't have too much contact with non-Jewish people, and so I didn't know too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever have discussions at home about what was happening in Austria, for instance, or in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we knew. We knew. Were discussing that, but [SIGHS] you couldn't help it. Like, I always was discussing with my mother, why don't you go where your whole family is? Your whole family lives in Vancouver. Why don't you go? Why did you stay here? She said they came to this country from-- because from a small city because of poverty. And I married well. And business is good. And my two sons came into the business. I cannot leave everything and leave. One day, we'll go just for a visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what year approximately these discussions took place?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- were your parents making any plans for the future-- for safety for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They were too much involved in the business. That was the tragedy because they could have get the papers to come here to Canada with no problems. She had, I think, four or five brothers, sisters, and mother and father here, everybody in Vancouver. So there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your household aware of the intensity of Jewish prosecution in other countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no persecution, really. We didn't hear nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland was the first country where the-- stepped in, in right away to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when Nazi occupation--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --began?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. It was a Wednesday morning. I remember like now. It was around 10 o'clock in the morning when they came in, September the 5th, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember what I ate yesterday, but that I remember. But we-- they were greeting from us very nice. But the next day wasn't so nice anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe those two days for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one, the next days, after two days?

speaker: interviewer
text: The day of and the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day after-- huh? The day after, they came to my father. I remember like now. No, that was Wednesday when they came. And so Friday morning, knock on the door. Can I have your keys?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said, from what? You know, from your business. And they took the keys from our business. Our tragedy was very big because my father only believed in the merchandise and only in the business. He was a businessman. So home, we had nothing. you know. We had no money, no nothing. Everything we had was in the business. So then they took the keys-- all the businesses. They took the key, and they left. And that was the end of the business-- for beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: About a week later, they came back and put the-- they didn't know how to open-- was something-- they needed my father. My father was always scared to go because it was that tragedy already. So I went. And they opened the business and came-- and they put the commissar. Commissar was somebody who took care of-- and all the money, they were collecting. And I was working with her, with the woman. She was a German woman. And she took over the business. Before us, when I was-- she let me be there. That was a good thing because I could-- like, one day, I brought a painter. I said, the business is stealing everything. Whoever can, steal at night. So we brought-- I brought a painter, who put merchandise in the ceiling, painted over, and made the hole and hide something. So we had for after the war when we took it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was hidden up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Merchandise, like clothes for-- suits for mens, shirt for men. We had only good things, really, velvet, you know, better merchandise. And my father never went back to the business-- not my mother. I was the only one going there. And that was the end of it, the end of the business. After they came to the house and-- for silver. If we had fur coats, if we had better clothes, they took everything away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much longer after they confiscated the business, did they come to the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a week or two later. It was very rapidly going-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents give them what they wanted?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] If you didn't give-- so you know that there's no tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any warning that this was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Maybe they had warning, but you, you couldn't imagine that something like it would happen, for no reason that somebody would come to you. My father was in the business 30 years, or whatever-- that that would be taken away in the second without any warnings, nothing. So it's-- nobody-- nobody expected that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next, Mrs. Kaufman?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that-- just a moment. I have to think. After that was-- I was going for a while to the business and coming home. We were still living, I don't know, a month or two in Cracow. And next to Cracow in the mountains, they open a camp, Plaszow. No, it was a ghetto. I'm sorry, it was ghetto before. But that was not far from Plaszow. And we moved to the-- we moved-- we had to move to ghetto with another one-- two-bedroom apartment with another two families, one on the top of the other. And we made the best out of it. And there I got the job on the airport. So that wasn't too bad because I was never hungry there. Planes were coming and leaving. And I had to clean the office. And that German was very nice to me. He was giving me to eat, you know, giving me a piece of bread, or whatever. So that was good then. And at night, the trucks were taking us back to the ghetto. But after a while, they liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me backtrack for a moment, please. How were you notified that your family was to be transferred to the Cracow Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The came and took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any advance warning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to pack anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, just went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the-- when you entered the Cracow Ghetto, did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- I'm sorry to interrupt you. Maybe we had a little package in the end, every one of us. But I really don't remember how big the package was or what was in it. Both from there, we went to the ghetto. In the ghetto, there was not so much room. It was small apartment and a few families. And we lived there for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was living in this apartment with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I have to be honest with you, I don't remember the name. I just remember one girl, who I am very good friends with her still. She lives in New York. And her name is Paula Fishman after she married. We are very close because we spend half the time-- she give me as money for-- helped me to get the job at the airport. And we were both going to the airport and always, if I could bring her a piece of bread or something, I was bringing to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your brothers living with you in this apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was your mother and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two, three brother-- two-- three brothers, yeah, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their wives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sure. One wasn't married. The youngest wasn't married. He is younger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the truth, I don't remember. I really don't remember how we got it. But it was tough, you know. If we went out to, to-- I don't know. I really don't remember how was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have a job in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody was working in the ghetto, nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- September-- 1940, beginning of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that you were among the first transport of Jews in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Cracow was liquidated together. We all went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, let me rephrase that. Were you among the first group of Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: To leave-- to leave--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to, to go into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if we were the first one or the last ones, but [SIGHS] we-- I was surrounded only by Jews. So I don't know if they were there-- some before, some came after. But Cracow was liquidated from the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm going to stop the tape here. And we're going to continue on the next tape. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
